## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BP
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.
6	
7	The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.
8	
9	## Locked decisions
10	
11	- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
12	- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.

# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All five co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earned a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).

# jjezs_pace ₢BPAAG

## Character

Mechanical projection over a single-homed group; mirrors the colophon shape against a different bash owner.

## Goal

Generate the rbtd fixture-name consts from their canonical bash owner via the shared primitive and migrate consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.

## Shape

- Fixture names are canonically authored in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` and hand-copied into `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`. Single home exists — projectable without a tidy step.
- Feed the names through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + fixture stem); migrate manifest consumers and any inline fixture literals onto the generated consts.
- Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — fixture-name canonical author
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fixture hand-mirror

## What done looks like

Fixture consts are generated `RBTDGC_*`, not hand-written; consumers reference the generated names; Rust builds and the fast suite is green.

## COMPREHENSIVE FIXTURE-NAME FLOW ANALYSIS

### 1. **The shared BUK primitive `buz_emit_const`**

**Definition:** `/home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh:149-164`

**Signature:**
```bash
buz_emit_const() {
  zbuz_sentinel
  local -r z_name="${1:-}"
  local -r z_value="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_name}"  || buc_die "buz_emit_const: const name required"
  test -n "${z_value}" || buc_die "buz_emit_const: value required"
  printf 'pub const %s: &str = "%s";\n' "${z_name}" "${z_value}"
}
```

**What it does:** Takes exactly 2 arguments (const_name and value), emits one `pub const <name>: &str = "<value>";` line to stdout. Name and value are carried verbatim — no prefixing, no suffixing, no casing transformation. The function is a per-pair scalar interface; bash loops invoke this once per pair.

---

### 2. **The colophon projection precedent**

**Function:** `rbz_emit_colophon_consts()` in `/home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh:174-188`

**How it loops colophons through the primitive:**
```bash
buz_emit_colophon_consts() {
  zbuz_sentinel
  local -r z_add_prefix="${1:-}"
  local -r z_strip_prefix="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_add_prefix}"   || buc_die "buz_emit_colophon_consts: add prefix required"
  test -n "${z_strip_prefix}" || buc_die "buz_emit_colophon_consts: strip prefix required"

  local z_i=""
  for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
    local z_stem="${z_buz_varname_roll[z_i]#"${z_strip_prefix}"}"
    buz_emit_const "${z_add_prefix}${z_stem}" "${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}"
  done
}
```

**Wiring into the theurge build:** Called from `rbz_emit_consts()` at `/home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh:246-254`:
```bash
rbz_emit_consts() {
  zrbz_sentinel
  printf '%s\n' "// Generated by the theurge build — rbz colophons + rbcc constants. Do not edit."
  printf '%s\n' "// Regenerate: tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  buz_emit_colophon_consts "RBTDGC_" "RBZ_" || buc_die "rbz_emit_consts: colophon emit failed"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  rbcc_emit_consts || buc_die "rbz_emit_consts: rbcc emit failed"
}
```

The colophon section calls `buz_emit_colophon_consts "RBTDGC_" "RBZ_"` — this strips the `RBZ_` prefix from enroll varnames and adds `RBTDGC_` prefix. Example: enroll varname `RBZ_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE` → stem `CRUCIBLE_CHARGE` → const `RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE` with value from `z_buz_colophon_roll`.

---

### 3. **The rbcc projection precedent**

**Function:** `rbcc_emit_consts()` in `/home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbcc_Constants.sh:120-150`

**How it loops rbcc set through the primitive:**
```bash
rbcc_emit_consts() {
  printf '%s\n' "// RBCC constants (rbcc_Constants.sh single-homed set)"

  local z_name=""
  local z_stem=""
  local z_upper=""
  for z_name in \
    RBCC_moorings_dir    \
    RBCC_vessels_subdir  \
    RBCC_role_governor   \
    ... (18 items total)
  ; do
    z_stem="${z_name#RBCC_}"
    z_upper="$(printf '%s' "${z_stem}" | tr '[:lower:]' '[:upper:]')"
    buz_emit_const "RBTDGC_${z_upper}" "${!z_name}"
  done
}
```

**Key difference from colophons:** rbcc directly iterates a hardcoded list of RBCC variable names, extracts stems, **uppercases** them via `tr`, then calls `buz_emit_const` with `RBTDGC_` prefix + uppercased stem. Values come from bash variable expansion (`"${!z_name}"`). Example: `RBCC_moorings_dir` → stem `moorings_dir` → upper `MOORINGS_DIR` → const `RBTDGC_MOORINGS_DIR`.

**Assembly into generated file:** From `rbz_emit_consts()` in rbz_zipper.sh:246-254, the combiner emits:
1. Banner comment
2. `buz_emit_colophon_consts "RBTDGC_" "RBZ_"` section (lines 251)

```rust
pub fn rbtdrm_verify(manifest: &str, fixture: &str) -> Result<(), String> {
    let required = match rbtdrm_required_colophons(fixture) {
        Some(r) => r,
        None => {
            return Err(format!(
                "rbtd: unknown fixture '{}' — not registered in manifest",
                fixture
            ));
        }
    };
    for colophon in required {
        let found = manifest.split_whitespace().any(|token| token == *colophon);
        if !found {
            return Err(format!(
                "rbtd: colophon '{}' not found in zipper manifest (fixture '{}')",
                colophon, fixture
            ));
        }
    }
    Ok(())
}
```

- Empty manifests fail (rbtdtm_rejects_empty_manifest)

pub fn rbtdrm_verify(manifest: &str, fixture: &str) -> Result<(), String> {
    let required = match rbtdrm_required_colophons(fixture) {
        Some(r) => r,
        None => {
            return Err(format!(
                "rbtd: unknown fixture '{}' — not registered in manifest",
                fixture

        if !found {
            return Err(format!(
                "rbtd: colophon '{}' not found in zipper manifest (fixture '{}')",
                colophon, fixture
            ));
        }
    }
    Ok(())
}
```

       z_upper="$(printf '%s' "${z_name}" | tr '[:lower:]' '[:upper:]' | tr '-' '_')"
       buz_emit_const "RBTDGC_${z_upper}" "${!z_name}"
     done
   }
   ```

2. **Call from rbz_emit_consts()** at rbz_zipper.sh:246-254:
   ```bash
   buz_emit_colophon_consts "RBTDGC_" "RBZ_"
   printf '%s\n' ""
   rbcc_emit_consts
   printf '%s\n' ""
   rbfx_emit_consts  # <— add new group
   ```

3. **Wire furnish:** Source the new module in rbte_cli.sh:43-45 before zrbz_kindle.

4. **Test coverage:** Add unit tests in rbtdtm_manifest.rs to verify projected consts match hand-written ones (e.g., `rbtdtm_fixtures_match_generated()`).

**Automation:** Once wired, the build and qualification gates **automatically** regenerate the file and verify freshness — no per-group build setup needed.

---

## FLAG: STRUCTURAL DIVERGENCE

**The plan to "mirror the colophon shape" for fixture names is NOT directly applicable.**

**Why:** 
- **Colophons:** Single-homed in bash `buz_enroll` calls with varname stems, looped via `buz_emit_colophon_consts` → Rust consts
- **RBCC set:** Single-homed in rbcc_Constants.sh hardcoded list, looped via `rbcc_emit_consts` → Rust consts
- **Fixture names:** **Distributed across multiple bash suite arrays**, string literals with **no single canonical author** in bash

**To project fixture names "without a tidy step":**
- Fixture names would need to be **extracted into a single bash array** (e.g., `RBCC_FIXTURES_ARRAY=("tadmor" "srjcl" ... )`)
- A projection function `rbfx_emit_consts()` would loop over it and call `buz_emit_const` per pair
- The bash suite arrays would reference this central array to avoid duplication
- **This refactoring is prerequisite** — the current state (string literals embedded in suite arrays) cannot be cleanly projected

**Current state:** Fixture names in rbtdrm_manifest.rs are **hand-maintained**, **not generated**. They are static consts that must be manually kept in sync with rbte_engine.sh suite definitions. The `rbtdrm_verify()` runtime check serves as a drift detector (test coverage ensures codegen toolchain consts match manifest hand-written).

# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE Engine - Theurge test engine implementation module
#
# BCG module providing kindle/sentinel/public functions for theurge
# Rust build, test, and orchestration commands.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZRBTE_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBTE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle

zrbte_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbte already kindled"

  local z_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

  readonly RBTE_MANIFEST="${z_dir}/Cargo.toml"
  readonly ZRBTE_BINARY="${z_dir}/target/debug/rbtd"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
  # but creates no GCP project per run.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "canonical-invest"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Dogfight — standing-depot cloud-build viability probe. Sibling to skirmish
  # in the operator-precondition family (reuses a hand-levied depot, no levy,
  # no unmake) but charges NO crucible: it proves only the cloud-build → summon
  # → run path yields a runnable artifact. canonical-invest leads exactly as it
  # does in skirmish — re-mantling the governor and divest/re-investing
  # retriever + director so the dogfight fixture finds fresh credentials; the
  # dogfight fixture itself stays crucible-free. OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a
  # canonical depot already levied (the standing-depot setup skirmish assumes).
  ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT=(
    "canonical-invest"
    "dogfight"
  )
  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".
  ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR=(
    "kludge-tadmor"
    "tadmor"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

# zrbte_codegen() - Refresh all zipper-derived artifacts before compiling.
# Write-on-change, so unchanged files keep their mtime and cargo skips rebuilds.
# The build is the sole producer — there is no standalone generate command.
zrbte_codegen() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  rbz_generate_context "${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}" "${RBCC_tabtarget_context_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to generate tabtarget context"
  rbz_generate_consts "${RBCC_rbtdgc_consts_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to generate colophon consts"
}

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    skirmish) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH[*]}" ;;
    dogfight) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT[*]}" ;;
    tadmor)   echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet|skirmish|dogfight|tadmor)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"
}

rbte_test() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Testing theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo test --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo test failed"
  buc_success "All theurge tests passed"
}

rbte_run() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  test -n "${z_fixture}" || buc_die "No fixture — pass one as the folio (e.g. rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh tadmor)"

  zrbte_build_binary

  buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
}

rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

  local z_fixture_list
  z_fixture_list="$(zrbte_resolve_suite "${z_suite}")"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_count=0
  local z_fixture
  for z_fixture in ${z_fixture_list}; do
    buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
    "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))
  done

  buc_success "Suite '${z_suite}' complete (${z_count} fixtures)"
}

rbte_single() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_fixture="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  local z_case="${1:-}"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" ${z_fixture:+"${z_fixture}"} ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}
}

I've completed the critical review you asked for, and I verified the load-bearing claims myself rather than trusting the survey. There's a substantive problem with this pace's premise — it doesn't hold up.

## Review of ₢BPAAG (fixture-codegen-projection)

**The docket's central claim is false.** It states:

> Fixture names are canonically authored in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` and hand-copied into `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`. **Single home exists — projectable without a tidy step.**

What's actually in the tree (verified directly):

1. **No single bash home.** Fixture names live as bare string literals scattered across **eight** suite-membership arrays in `rbte_engine.sh:42-115` (`ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST`, `_SERVICE`, `_CRUCIBLE`, `_COMPLETE`, `_GAUNTLET`, `_SKIRMISH`, `_DOGFIGHT`, `_TADMOR`), with heavy overlap. There is no canonical fixture *list* in bash — these are composition lists, each repeating names.

2. **Bash isn't even the author.** The actual fixture *definitions* — the runnable entities — live in the Rust registry `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2358`), whose `rbtdre_Fixture` structs already carry `name: RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*`. And the **four `calibrant-*` fixtures exist only in Rust** — they appear in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` but in *no* bash suite array. So bash isn't even a superset, let alone the canonical owner. If anything, **Rust is the canonical home** and the bash arrays are the downstream consumers.

3. **This pace violates the heat's own locked decision.** The paddock states:

   > Constant-projection scope: **single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are **excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.**

   Fixture-name identity is distributed across bash suite arrays *and* the Rust registry/manifest. By the heat's own rule, fixtures are **"out"** until a canonical-author decision is made. The docket's "without a tidy step" framing is precisely the misclassification the locked decision was written to catch — it treated fixtures like colophons/rbcc (genuinely single-homed) when they're actually in the scattered category that ₢BPAAI's operation-verbs/container-roles had to *tidy first*.

**Minor:** the docket's "keep the runtime drift check alive" refers to `rbtdrm_verify`, which checks *colophon* presence per fixture — it does not check fixture-name consistency, so that line is slightly misaimed for this pace anyway.

**Bottom line:** as docketed, this is not a mechanical mirror of the colophon shape. Mounting it as-written would either stall immediately (no single source to loop over) or quietly assert bash ownership over names Rust actually defines. Pace scope is your call, so I'm stopping here to ask rather than picking a direction.

Here's the full picture, with all claims now verified:

## Grooming verdict on ₣BP

**₢BPAAG (fixture projection) does not make sense in context. Recommend dropping it.** Three independent reasons:

1. **The redundancy it claims to fix doesn't exist.** The docket says fixture names are "hand-copied into `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`" and kept honest by a "runtime drift check." Verified false: there is **no** drift check tying bash fixture names to the Rust `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` consts. `rbtdtm_manifest.rs` only exercises `rbtdrm_verify`, which checks *colophons-per-fixture*. The bash suite-array literals and the Rust name consts are fully independent — there's nothing being "kept in sync" to collapse.

2. **It inverts authority against the project's trajectory.** Rust is the canonical fixture home: `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2358`) *defines* fixtures, the structs reference `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*`, and 4 `calibrant-*` fixtures exist **only** in Rust. The bash suite arrays are composition lists that pass name-strings as CLI args to the theurge binary, which validates them against its own registry. Projecting bash→Rust would make **bash the source of fixture identity** — exactly backwards from "pull test details into theurge." So your instinct ("should fixture names even be in bash?") is right: fixture *identity* shouldn't be bash-owned, and isn't today.

3. **It violates ₣BP's own locked decision** — "single canonical bash author, or out; distributed-identity constants are excluded." Fixture identity is distributed across 8 bash suite arrays *and* the Rust registry. It's an "out."

**The other paces survive the cut cleanly:**
- **₢BPAAH** (operation verbs + container roles) — genuinely single-homed in `rbcc_Constants.sh` after ₢BPAAI's tidy. Sound.
- **₢BPAAF** (build-wiring + diff-gate, retire `rbtdrm_verify`) — sound and **independent of fixtures**; `rbtdrm_verify` is about colophons, so dropping AAG doesn't touch it. Both done — AAG dropped (commit `89e6b93`), AAF reslated (the "fixtures" mention is gone from its regenerated-set enumeration).

## The itch, restated cleanly

Here's the concept on its own, stripped of the projection discussion:

**The situation today.** Theurge's suites (fast/service/crucible/complete/gauntlet/skirmish/dogfight/tadmor) are composed in bash — `rbte_engine.sh:42-115` has eight arrays like:

```bash
ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
```

Those are raw fixture-name string literals. The *same* fixture names are the canonical identities over in Rust (`RBTDRC_FIXTURES` / `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*`). So a fixture name lives in two languages with **nothing guarding that they agree** — fat-finger "tadmor" → "tadmore" in a bash suite and the only signal is a runtime "unknown fixture" failure when that suite runs.

**Why projection (the dropped AAG) was the wrong fix.** It would have made bash the *author* of fixture identity and generated the Rust names from it — pushing ownership the wrong way, since Rust already owns what a fixture *is*.

**The right-direction fix (the itch).** Move *suite composition itself* into theurge. Rust already owns fixture identity; let it also own which fixtures belong to which suite — e.g. a `suite → &[&Fixture]` table in the crate. Then:
- bash's `rbte_suite` passes only the **suite name** to the binary (it already passes a suite name; it just wouldn't pre-expand the fixture list),
- the eight bash arrays and all their literal fixture names **disappear**,
- there's exactly one home for "what fixtures exist" and "what's in each suite," both in theurge.

This is the same principle ₣BJ's paddock states outright — *"theurge should be a clean consumer of operator tabtargets, never an owner... pull test details onto contract surfaces."* It belongs in **₣BJ (testing-simplification)**, not ₣BP (projection cleanup) — it's a consolidation move, not a bash→Rust const projection.

One caveat to flag before you decide where it lands: bash currently *iterates* the expanded fixture list (`rbte_suite` loops `for z_fixture in ...` and runs the binary once per fixture, line 214-218). Moving composition into Rust means the binary takes a suite name and loops internally — a real behavioral shift in where the per-fixture loop lives, not a pure lift. Worth a sentence in whatever docket captures it.

Want me to file it as an itch, or slate it directly onto ₣BJ?

aiTitle: Review Opus-curated plan for alignment and efficiency

content.text: Strong hits. Two things jump out that bear directly on your "I thought we already had that" — and they may actually point the *opposite* way from the itch I proposed. Let me read the decisive ones.

## Character

Mechanical Rust pace. Replace dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` with `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` per RCG terminal exclusivity — `c` is owned by crucible). Register four calibrant fixtures with deterministic verdicts; pin behavior in `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Self-contained — bash driver pace consumes this but does not land here.

## Scope

- Delete `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` and its `lib.rs` declaration; add `rbtdrl_calibrant` module + `rbtdtl_calibrant` test module.
- Four fixtures registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` (fixture-name consts), `rbtdrc_fixture_disposition`, and `rbtdrc_sections_for_fixture`:
  - `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent): 4 cases — `pass`, `fail`, `skip`, `pass_with_output` (writes `output.txt`).
  - `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent): two sections — §A `[pass, fail, not_reached]` (intra-section), §B `[not_reached]` (inter-section).
  - `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing): 2 cases — case 1 probe `Ok` then `Pass`; case 2 probe hardcoded `Err` (case body unreachable).
  - `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent): single case writes `ran.sentinel` into its case `dir`.
- All four `rbtdrm_required_colophons` entries: `Some(&[])` (calibrant cases never shell out).
- `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs` unit tests pin per-case verdict, sentinel write/non-write, disposition tags, sections registration, manifest entries.

## Locked decisions

- **Case-written file**: `output.txt` — distinct from engine-auto `trace.txt`. Only `pass_with_output` writes it; engine writes `trace.txt` for every case implicitly.
- **Sentinel mechanism**: not_reached cases and `calibrant-sentinel` write `ran.sentinel` into their case temp dir (`dir.join("ran.sentinel")`). Bash side (Pace 2) asserts presence/absence by globbing under the `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` it set for the rbtd invocation. No project-relative paths — hygiene matters.
- **Probe-Err mechanism**: `calibrant-progressing` case 2's `check` fn is hardcoded `Err`. No env-var or file coupling. Single fixture run exercises both `Ok` and `Err` probe paths deterministically.
- **Suite is bash composition**: no `RBTDRM_SUITE_CALIBRANT` constant. Suite-level fail-fast comes from `rbte_engine.sh` `set -euo pipefail` per existing pattern. Suite tabtarget (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.calibrant.sh`) defers to Pace 2 alongside its testbench consumer.
- **No per-fixture tabtargets**: calibrant fixtures are framework-test plumbing, not operator workflows. The bash testbench (Pace 2) invokes the `rbtd` binary directly — tabtargets are thin shells not part of the contract under test. Deviates from BBAAe's per-fixture pattern; justified because pristine/canonical are operator-facing, calibrant isn't.

You didn't miss anything — I did. The strategy you're remembering is real, settled, and recent, and the itch I floated last turn actually **contradicts** it. Here's the reconciliation.

## The strategy already exists — and it's a boundary, not "everything into theurge"

Two locked decisions draw the line, and they agree:

1. **₣BJ paddock** (testing-simplification): *"Theurge should be a clean consumer of operator tabtargets, never an owner of commands, never a reacher into internals."*

2. **₢BBAAh locked decision** (calibrant-fixture-foundation, landed in ₣BB) — explicit and unambiguous:
   > **"Suite is bash composition: no `RBTDRM_SUITE_CALIBRANT` constant. Suite-level fail-fast comes from `rbte_engine.sh` `set -euo pipefail` per existing pattern."**

So the division of labor was *deliberately* settled:

| Concern | Owner |
|---|---|
| Fixture **identity** (`RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*`) | Rust / theurge |
| Fixture **behavior** — cases, disposition, sections, required-colophons | Rust / theurge |
| Required-colophon **coverage** (exhaustive subset check) | Rust / theurge — ₣BC `₢BCAAB` |
| **Suite composition** — which fixtures group into fast/service/crucible | **bash, on purpose** (`₢BBAAh`) |

## Where I was wrong

Last turn I proposed an itch to "move suite composition into theurge so Rust owns which fixtures are in which suite." That is precisely the thing `₢BBAAh` **locked against** — suites are bash composition by design, with `set -e` as the fail-fast. My itch would reverse a deliberate decision. **Retract it** — it was my error, not a gap you missed.

The consequence: the bash suite arrays holding literal fixture-name strings are **not a redundancy/wart**. They're the bash-side expression of suite membership — the intended cross-language boundary. A fixture name living as both a bash literal *and* a Rust const is the boundary working as designed; the guard is the binary rejecting an unknown fixture name at runtime (`rbte_run`/`rbte_suite` → `rbtd` → `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` lookup). No static drift-check is wanted there, which is exactly why ₣BC's coverage work targets *colophons*, not fixture names.

## So what was the actual "miss"?

It was in the **curation of AAG itself**, now fixed. AAG (Opus 4.7) was drafted as if fixtures were just-another-projectable-group like colophons — but `₢BBAAh` and ₣BJ had already settled fixtures as Rust-canonical with bash-composed suites. AAG never reconciled against that. **Dropping AAG aligns ₣BP with the already-settled strategy.** No new pace, no itch — the boundary you remember is intact, and ₣BP now respects it.

Net of this whole detour: AAG dropped ✓, AAF reslated to not claim fixtures ✓, and the suite-composition itch withdrawn. ₣BP's remaining real work is ₢BPAAH (verbs/roles projection) and ₢BPAAF (build-wiring + diff-gate) — both sound.

content.text:
Read the binary. Now I can think about this concretely rather than abstractly, and I'll commit to a position.

## Verdict: I agree with you. Supersede BBAAh.

Your lean is the *consistent* one, and BBAAh is the outlier — not because it was wrong then, but because of *why* it was decided. Here's the reasoning, grounded in what the binary actually does.

### BBAAh was a conformance call, not a principled ownership decision

Its exact wording: *"Suite is bash composition … set -euo pipefail **per existing pattern**."* That "per existing pattern" is the tell. BBAAh landed inside ₣BB (release-qualification), where scope discipline is the whole point — the calibrant work needed a suite tabtarget and chose **not to redesign the suite mechanism mid-release-qual**. It matched what existed. It never argued bash is the *right* owner of suite knowledge. So it's exactly the kind of circumstantial decision that's fair to revisit once the surrounding principle sharpens — which is what ₣BP has been doing for two days.

### Why your model is actively better (not just equally good)

1. **It's the same principle ₣BP already runs on.** Every pace this heat has landed says: one canonical owner, kill cross-language co-maintenance. Fixtures are *already* Rust-canonical (`RBTDRC_FIXTURES`). A suite is just a named set of fixtures — and today that set is expressed as bash string literals that duplicate the Rust fixture names with no guard. Putting the set where the fixtures live is the heat's own logic, finished.

2. **Membership becomes compile-time checked.** A Rust suite registry references the fixture statics by `&'static Fixture`. A typo'd or deleted fixture *won't compile*. Today a bad name in a bash array fails only when that suite runs (`rbtdrm_verify` → runtime "unknown fixture"). Strictly stronger.

3. **₣BJ supports this, doesn't forbid it.** ₣BJ's "theurge is a consumer, never an owner" targeted theurge owning an *operator command surface* (the `rbtd-ap` colophon family) and reaching into module internals. Suite composition is *test-orchestration knowledge* — precisely what theurge should own. The operator still drives via tabtargets; only the "which fixtures" knowledge moves. No conflict.

4. **The run-boundary guards get *more* correct.** Right now `main.rs:128` runs the dirty-tree guard on **every** fixture invocation (N times per suite) — it only passes on fixture 2+ because committing fixtures happen to re-clean the tree. A real suite mode runs that guard **once**, at the true suite boundary. Same for root allocation (`rbtdb_allocate_roots`) and fail-fast: an in-process loop gives ordered fail-fast with which-fixture-failed reporting, richer than bash `set -e`.

### The one real risk, and the care-points

Today each fixture is a **separate process** (`rbte_suite` loops, invoking the binary per fixture). Moving the loop into Rust means **one process runs all fixtures**. That's the load-bearing change, and it looks safe but must be verified at implementation:

- **Context model is already built for re-entry** — `rbtdrc_set_context`/`rbtdrc_take_context` set-and-*remove* per fixture, so sequential set→run→take→set→run→take in one process is the intended shape. No persistent global I can see; cwd and env are read, not mutated.
- **Care-point: teardown-on-failure.** Crucible `quench` must run even when a case fails mid-fixture, inside the loop. `rbtdre_run_fixture` already brackets charge/quench around cases — confirm it holds on the failure path before relying on it.
- **Care-point: panic ≠ failure.** A single-process suite means a Rust panic aborts the whole run (today the process boundary contains it). That's arguably *correct* (fail loud), but quench-on-panic for a charged crucible needs a deliberate answer.

### Concrete shape

- **Rust:** a suite registry — `suite-name → &[&'static Fixture]` — referencing the same statics `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` already holds. A new binary mode `rbtd suite <manifest> <suite-name>` loops fixtures with per-fixture charge/run/quench and aggregate summary. The single-fixture and `single` modes stay for `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc`.
- **Bash collapses:** the 8 `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays and `zrbte_resolve_suite`'s case statement (`rbte_engine.sh:42-115, 151-164`) **delete**; `rbte_suite` becomes a one-line pass-through of the imprint to the binary. The rich suite-rationale comments (gauntlet state-walk, skirmish/dogfight operator preconditions) move to Rust doc-comments on the registry — better co-located than now.
- **Result:** suite names exist *only* as tabtarget imprints (`rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` → `…skirmish.sh`). Zero suite names, zero fixture lists in bash. Exactly what you described.

### Where it lands

This isn't ₣BP's mechanical const-projection — it's a small architecture move, so I'd **not** smuggle it in here. Best home is **₣BJ (testing-simplification)** — its paddock literally says "pull test details onto contract surfaces; theurge as clean consumer," and this is that. The pace should explicitly note it **supersedes BBAAh's "suite is bash composition" decision** so the reversal is on the record.

Want me to slate it onto ₣BJ with that shape and the supersedes-note, or would you rather I draft it as an itch first so you can shape scope?

# jjezs_slate theurge-owns-suite-composition

## Character

Architecture move requiring judgment — one load-bearing risk (single-process fixture isolation) gates an otherwise mechanical collapse.

## Goal

Make theurge the sole owner of suite→fixture composition. Suite names survive only as tabtarget imprints; no suite name or fixture list appears anywhere in bash.

## Locked constraints

- Supersedes the "suite is bash composition" decision (₢BBAAh, ₣BB) — a release-qual conformance call, not a principled ownership stance.
- Suite membership is expressed in Rust by reference to the existing `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` statics, so a bad member fails at compile time — not a name-string list mirrored across the boundary.
- The dirty-tree guard and per-run root allocation move to once-per-suite; today they run once per fixture.

## Shape

- Rust gains a suite registry and the binary a suite mode (suite name in, its fixtures run); the single-fixture and `single` modes stay. Bash's suite arrays, the suite-resolve, and the per-fixture loop collapse to an imprint pass-through; the suite-rationale comments (gauntlet state-walk, skirmish/dogfight operator preconditions) migrate to Rust doc on the registry.
- Load-bearing risk: the per-fixture loop becomes one process instead of N. Confirm crucible quench runs on the case-failure path, and settle quench-on-panic, before relying on the in-process loop.

# jjezs_slate theurge-owns-suite-composition

## Character

Architecture move requiring judgment — one load-bearing risk (single-process fixture isolation) gates an otherwise mechanical collapse.

## Goal

Make theurge the sole owner of suite→fixture composition. Suite names survive only as tabtarget imprints; no suite name or fixture list appears anywhere in bash.

## Locked constraints

- Supersedes the "suite is bash composition" decision (₢BBAAh, ₣BB) — a release-qual conformance call, not a principled ownership stance.
- Suite membership is expressed in Rust by reference to the existing `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` statics, so a bad member fails at compile time — not a name-string list mirrored across the boundary.
- The dirty-tree guard and per-run root allocation move to once-per-suite; today they run once per fixture.

## Shape

- Rust gains a suite registry and the binary a suite mode (suite name in, its fixtures run); the single-fixture and `single` modes stay. Bash's suite arrays, the suite-resolve, and the per-fixture loop collapse to an imprint pass-through; the suite-rationale comments (gauntlet state-walk, skirmish/dogfight operator preconditions) migrate to Rust doc on the registry.
- Load-bearing risk: the per-fixture loop becomes one process instead of N. Confirm crucible quench runs on the case-failure path, and settle quench-on-panic, before relying on the in-process loop.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — suite arrays, resolve, `rbte_suite` loop
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` — binary modes, dirty-tree guard, root allocation
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs` — `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` fixture statics
- the `rbw-ts.TestSuite.*` suite tabtargets — imprint channel

Suite composition lives only in theurge; grep finds no suite names or fixture lists in bash; suite tabtargets pass only their imprint; a typo'd suite member fails compilation; all suites run green.
